et des motifs

Conseil(s) du (de la/des) demandeur(e)(s) d'asile

N/A
Conseil du (de la) ministre
REASONS FOR DECISION
[1] The claimant, XXXX XXXX, arrived in Canada on XXXX XXXX, 2008 to make a refugee claim. A Refugee Protection Division denied his claim in an October 17, 2012 decision. Subsequently, a Federal Court decision of Justice Noel of September 10, 2013 quashed the decision and ordered a redetermination.
ALLEGATIONS
[2] The claimant is a 58-year-old ethnic Jewish man from XXXX in western Ukraine, about 70 kilometres west of L'viv. He actually worked in Turka, about 80 kilometres away, which was a 90-minute commute. He had come to Canada in 1998 for a week to buy dentistry equipment.
[3] He fears Ukrainian nationalists who beat him in 2007 as he was returning from work. After the incident, he was hospitalized for six days. He did not report the attack immediately to police. Subsequently, he was interviewed by police while hospitalized who said they would try to sort out the situation. However, he said nothing was ever done. He never complained about this inaction to any higher police authority or contacted a lawyer because he said it would create more problems. His home's front door was defaced in November 2007 and his car was stolen the next month. He never reported those incidents to police. He left Ukraine on XXXX XXXX, 2008, purchased a false passport and traveled to Canada.
[4] In his December 15, 2008 Personal Information Form (PIF) he stated he would be beaten or killed by Ukrainian nationalists. He said Kiev would not be a safe or viable Internal Flight Alternative (IFA) location for him. He said he would have to register his residence and would have difficulty finding an apartment and employment; he added he had no friends or family in Kiev. He said people would know that he is Jewish because of his accent. The claimant said he was harassed and bullied because of his Jewish ethnicity since childhood but could not give an explanation why this assertion was omitted from his PIF.
IDENTITY
[5] The claimant's national identity is established by his testimony in the Ukrainian language and a copies of various identity documents.1
ANALYSIS
[6] The main issue is the objective country conditions that the claimant would face today as an ethnic Jewish man who has not lived in the Ukraine since November 2008. Counsel submitted in oral and written submissions that there were high levels of anti-Jewish incidents in the country and that there was a dramatic rise in anti-Semitic events overall and no part of Ukraine was safe making a Kiev IFA untenable.
[7] I considered the present and prospective risk for the claimant who has not lived in the Ukraine for almost a decade. There is conflicting information on country conditions when considering the more recent documentary evidence. Counsel submitted that his package on documentary evidence in Exhibit 3 demonstrates a surge in anti-Semitic violence. This is contradicted by Exhibit 7,2 the U.S. Department of State's Religious Freedom Report for 2017, which relies on information provided by the National Minority Rights Monitoring Group (NMRMG), an NGO supported by the Euro-Jewish Congress and the Association of Jewish Organizations and Communities.
[8] The NMRMG stated that "...no anti-Semitic violence was recorded during the year, compared with one case in 2016, one case in 2015, four cases in 2014, and four in 2013."3 There are an estimated 360,000 Jews in the Ukraine according to the European Jewish Congress.4 There are instances of anti-Semitic vandalism. "The NMRMG reported 24 cases of anti-Semitic vandalism during the year [2017], compared with 19 in 2016 and 22 in 2015."5
[9] The main source of counsel's contention that there is a surge of anti-Semitic violence in the Ukraine is the Israeli government's Ministry for Diaspora Affairs, which reported that anti-Semitic violence doubled from 2016 to 2017 and surpassed the tally for all the incidents reported throughout the region combined.6 The Ministry estimates there were more than 130 incidents of anti-Semitism including violent assaults.7 The World Jewish Congress reports that the rate of anti-Semitic incidents appears to be escalating.8 Its figure of 130 incidents are based on those of the Israeli Ministry.9
[10] However, the totals by Israel's Ministry for Diaspora Affairs are disputed in a report by a researcher of anti-Semitism from the Ukraine that said the Israeli report was "flawed and amateurish."10 The researcher, Vyacheslav Likhachov,11 who is affiliated with the Va'ad Association of Jewish Communities and Organizations of Ukraine, suggested that the [Ministry] report authors merely summed up the incidents they found reported online, in a "blatant lack of professionalism and violation of all standards of hate crimes documentation, which are guided by professional monitoring groups, both in Ukraine and in the world," he said.12
[11] The panel prefers the evidence provided by local and regional Jewish minority rights monitoring groups cited by the U.S. Department of State Religious Freedom Report for 2017 including the NMRMG, an NGO supported by the Euro-Jewish Congress and the Association of Jewish Organizations and Communities and a researcher with the Va'ad Association of Jewish Communities and Organizations of Ukraine. I find the hate crimes documentation provided by these groups was more thorough and rigorous for the reasons outlined above by the Va'ad Association of Jewish Communities and Organizations.13 I conclude that there are isolated incidents of anti-Semitic violence in the Ukraine coupled with more documented occurrences of anti-Semitic vandalism.
[12] In Sukhraj Singh,14 the Federal Court recognized that the documentary evidence in a particular case may not be unequivocal as to the political situation in a country, even contradictory in some areas. These contradictions include the fact that, coupled with instances of anti-Semitic vandalism, the country has a Jewish Prime Minister.15 I conclude the evidence is sufficient to support my conclusion that although there are unfortunate incidents of hateful anti-Semitic incidents in the Ukraine it is not so pervasive that it would amount to persecution for the claimant before me.
[13] I do not find the claimant's fear of persecution is objectively well-founded. As part of my blended assessment of well-foundedness I considered other factors such as the passage of almost a decade and on a balance of probabilities the resultant lack of continued interest in the claimant by any past agent of persecution in concluding there is no serious possibility of persecution should the claimant return to the Ukraine.
CONCLUSION
[14] For these reasons, and after consideration of all of the evidence and submissions, I find that the claimant, XXXX XXXX, is not a Convention refugee or a person in need of protection, pursuant to s. 96 or s.97.1 of IRPA.16
[15] Therefore, I reject the claim.
(signed)
"L. Colle"
L. Colle
September 4, 2018
Date
1 Exhibits 1 and 4.
2 Exhibit 7, U.S. Department of State, Bureau of Democracy, Human Rights and Labor, International Religious Freedom Report for 2017.
3 Ibid, at p. 10.
4 Exhibit 3, at pp. 38 & 39.
5 Exhibit 7, at p. 14.
6 Exhibit 3, at p. 38.
7 Ibid.

11 Also spelled Likhachev.
12 Exhibit 3, at p. 39.
13 Ibid.
14 Sing, Sukhrarj v. M.C.I (F.C.T.D., no. IMM-448-97), Lufty, December 31, 1997.
15 Exhibit 3, at p. 40.
16 Immigration and Refugee Protection Act, S.C.., 2001, c 27.
